question:
Will Alexander Albon finish on the podium at the 2025 F1 Qatar Grand Prix?

Description: This market will resolve to “Yes” if the listed driver finishes within the top three positions in the official "Final Classification" for the F1 Qatar Grand Prix, scheduled for Nov 30, 2025. Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No.”

The "Final Classification" is published by the FIA following the conclusion of the race and includes any applied time penalties and official adjustments. It is typically released 30-60 minutes after the race ends.

Disqualifications or changes made after the publication of the "Final Classification" will not affect market resolution. The timing of the podium ceremony does not determine the result for this market — only the FIA's published classification will be used to resolve this market.

If the Qatar Grand Prix is postponed, this market will remain open until the event has been completed. If the Qatar Grand Prix is permanently canceled, this market will resolve 50-50.

Resolution source: https://www.formula1.com/en/results/2025/races

evidences.title: Every Qatar Grand Prix F1 Winner | Complete List - F1 History
evidences.content: After a one-year absence due to the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Qatar, the Grand Prix returned in 2023 under a long-term agreement. The weekend featured a sprint format, adding further intensity to an already demanding circuit. Verstappen entered the weekend on the brink of another world title and duly sealed the championship during the sprint event. In the Grand Prix itself, he converted pole position into victory with a controlled drive under challenging, hot conditions that tested drivers physically. The win underlined Red Bull’s dominance in 2023 and cemented Qatar as the site of Verstappen’s third consecutive world championship triumph.2024 – Max Verstappen The 2024 edition once again featured a sprint weekend and added another chapter to Verstappen’s growing Lusail legacy. evidences.title: Instagram
evidences.content: But how have the pair compared beyond Baku? 🧐 Over the last 10 races (excluding Spain, Canada & Austria), Sainz has generally had the upper hand in qualifying (ave. -0.145s) while Albon has had superior pace on race day by an average delta of 0.094s. 🇭🇺 The biggest difference between the pair in quali, aside from in Baku, came in Hungary where, although both were really limited by the car’s overall poor pace, Albon exited in Q1 and was P20, while Sainz’s own Q1 lap was 0.571s quicker - enough to put him into Q2 and P13. 🇧🇪 On a Sunday, again aside from Baku, the biggest difference has swung in Albon’s favour. He was 0.699s quicker in Belgium as he didn’t put a foot wrong in tricky mixed conditions, while Sainz suffered a “costly setup change” that limited his pace. [...] Is Sainz really quicker than Albon? 🤔 🏆 Carlos Sainz accomplished a big step on Williams’ journey back to the top with his podium in Baku, and although he was the one to stand on the rostrum, it really was a team achievement which reflected in how they all, including Albon, celebrated! But on a personal note for Sainz, it marked a shift in recent form for him and ended a six-race long point-less run… It was also...

evidences.title: 'We should have scored points' – Albon and Sainz reflect on difficult 2025 Formula 1 Sao Paulo GP for Williams
evidences.content: When asked if it was a circuit which didn't suit the FW47, Albon added: “Maybe, but we need to understand why, because we talk a lot about next year, but we need to also understand why at weekends like this, we are so slow. "I think we were the slowest car this weekend on paper, and we’ve just been struggling.” Sainz could only claim P13, just one-second behind Albon, but was nursing damage after opening corner contact with Lewis Hamilton's Ferrari. Williams still remain P5 in the Teams' Championship despite non-scoring, but sit just 29 points clear of Racing Bulls with three Grands Prix and a Sprint remaining, as both Lawson and Isack Hadjar finished inside the top 10 in Brazil. "I think I just got squeezed by Lewis on the outside," said Sainz. [...] Both drivers failed to reach Q3 in Qualifying at Interlagos, with neither driver having taken any points from Saturday's earlier Sprint. The Williams duo generally lacked pace compared with many midfield teams due to the circuit's characteristics. In the 71-lap race, both drivers hovered on the edge of the top 10, with Albon finishing P11 but less than two seconds behind Liam Lawson, who led a train of cars to the chequered flag. “...

evidences.title: In F1’s tight midfield battle, Alex Albon – not Carlos Sainz – leads Williams’ charge - The Athletic
evidences.content: “A good example would be Japan. We were really weak in some corners around the lap, but on the rest of the lap, we’re actually not far away from the top teams.” Bahrain has been a circuit where Williams has struggled in recent years. The car tends to perform better in cooler temperatures. But in 2025, the team had the pace to be in the top 10, with Sainz qualifying eighth. Albon, though, missed out on Q3 for the first time this season. [...] Sainz knew 2025’s first quarter would be tricky as he learns more about the Williams car and becomes comfortable driving it. Historically, he has adapted well when changing teams, but moving from frontrunning Ferrari to a Williams car that often struggles to make Q3 is a big change. He even said he has needed to shed the muscle memory of what he learned at Ferrari when driving Williams’ FW47. [...] Sainz and Albon are working in tandem to help build Williams’ future and they have the potential to become a force to be reckoned with in the midfield. With a complete driver lineup capable of scoring regular points, Williams could capture the ‘best of the rest’ title behind the leading top four teams. The last time Williams finished as high as fi...

evidences.title: Facts, stats and trivia ahead of the 2025 Qatar Grand Prix
evidences.content: Had Norris kept his P2 finish, he would have been 30 points clear of team mate Oscar Piastri and 42 ahead of Red Bull rival Max Verstappen going into the final two rounds. Instead, he is a much-reduced 24 points ahead of Verstappen and Piastri, who are now equal on points. With 58 points still up for grabs across this weekend in Qatar (which features the Sprint format) and Abu Dhabi, it is still very much a three-way fight – and very much all to play for. As touched on above, Verstappen has form at the Lusail International Circuit, finishing second in the 2021 Grand Prix and triumphing in 2023 and 2024, though McLaren and Piastri have won the Sprints here over the last two years. evidences.title: Qatar Grand Prix
evidences.content: Other than returning in 2023, they race also became a sprint race that year, meaning, that they would now be required to drive half a race Saturday, before qualifying for the main race later that day. Since the sprint was introduced on the Lusail circuit, only one drive has actually won it, and that is the McLaren driver Oscar Piastri, who won it in his rookie season (2023). He also has 2/3 sprint pole positions on the circuit, only missing out in 202 to his teammate Lando Norris. On the other hand, one can argue that the Red Bull Racing driver Max Verstappen, is the one who has had most succes at the track. evidences.title: F1 to Have Mandatory Tire Limits at Qatar GP
evidences.content: Skip to Content # F1 to Have Mandatory Tire Limits at Qatar GP The move will require all drivers to make two pit stops during the race. By Phillip Horton Peter Fox//Getty Images Formula 1 tire supplier Pirelli has introduced maximum stint lengths at next weekend’s Qatar Grand Prix on the grounds of safety concerns. The high-speed high-energy Lusail Circuit puts maximum loads through the tires and, despite bringing its hardest compounds, Pirelli encountered problems at the venue in both 2023 and 2024. The issues encountered in 2023—namely, the aggressive nature of some curbs—prompted Pirelli to introduce maximum stint lengths of 18 laps, and this was subsequently resolved for 2024 after modifications were made to the curbs.

evidences.title: F1 limits drivers to just 25 laps per set of tyres for Qatar Grand Prix – RaceFans
evidences.content: Pirelli has made the decision despite nominating the hardest tyres in its range for the event. The Losail circuit, which features many medium-to-high speed corners, is one of the most demanding on the calendar for tyre performance. F1 has experienced problems with tyres at the circuit before. During the inaugural race in 2021 several drivers suffered tyre failures. When F1 returned for its second event at the track in 2023 Pirelli discovered during the weekend that faults were developing in their tyres’ sidewalls due to damage incurred on the track’s kerbs. They imposed a limit of 18 laps per stint, which required drivers to pit three times during the race. [...] They certainly aren’t gonna stop 3 times for no gain. 1. Profile Photo Esploratore (@esploratore1) 17th November 2025, 10:33 Ah, looks like only 18 laps for that 3-stop race, I remembered 20. 5. Coventry Climax 17th November 2025, 11:22 So Pirelli encountered these issues for the first time in 2021, they say. What a joke that statement alone is already: There’s been issues with them since they became the only supplier. Was tempted to say ‘sole supplier’, but frankly, even my shoes last longer and perform better. Then in...

evidences.content: LAS VEGAS GRAND PRIX 2025 LAS VEGAS Date 20 – 22 Nov Race distance 309.958 km Circuit length 6.201 km Number of laps 50 Las Vegas hosted two Formula 1 races in 1981 and 1982 on a temporary track next to the Caesars Palace hotel. The return of Formula 1 to the city took place in the heart of downtown Las Vegas – the new 6.12km, 14-turn street circuit winds its way past iconic locations like the aforementioned Caesars Palace, the Bellagio and the Venetian. Average speeds matched that of Monza, otherwise known as F1’s Temple of Speed’ and it provided plenty of action and overtaking. evidences.title: Is Williams F1's 'best of the rest' in 2025? Alex Albon's Imola drive ...
evidences.content: In hindsight, Albon thought he could have been more patient about attempting an overtake on Leclerc. Both Leclerc’s and Piastri’s tires were significantly older than Albon’s at that point. “At that point in the race, honestly, I was feeling so good,” Albon said. “I thought, ‘Oscar’s up ahead on one side, I can go after him and maybe get a P3’.” Williams finished the race in the top five on pure pace, and Albon battled McLaren and Ferrari on merit. Vowles noted in the team’s post-race recap, “We’ve been fast all year long and we are progressing relative to some giants; we were running on genuine merit up in a podium position.” [...] Advertisement “I’ve always told the team again, this is the year to do these mistakes,” Sainz said. “At the same time, it’s time to make a step.” He elaborated on that point: “Williams is not fighting for any world championships, but I do believe Williams has the potential to fight for race wins and championships in the future.” And Sainz isn’t wrong. The team is making steps with its car and the infrastructure. And it has a strong driver lineup in he and Albon. While teams are catching up as they bring upgrades, like this weekend in Imola, Williams i...

evidences.title: DHL Fastest Lap Award: 2025 Brazilian GP (Alex Albon / Williams)
evidences.content: Alex Alburn won his first DHL fastest lap award at 2025 on his way to a P11 finish at a dramatic Sa Paulo Grand Prix. A race in which Landon Norris extended his lead in the drivers championship and Maxappen went from a pit lane start to a podium finish. Alan had not had a good weekend at Interaros. He never quite got his setup right in the single hour of practice. P12 for his efforts and he finished in the same spot during spring qualifying, admitting to a lot of frustration and confusion in the Williams garage. In the short form race, luck was against him and Gabriel Bortletto's dramatic crash led to debris on the track. The Saura driver's front wing getting lodged under the tie driver's car. He was the last of the finishers.
